Preprocessing Example¶

This notebook walks through the gaze preprocessing pipeline one step at a time for a single session. It trims the first 2 minutes and the last 1 minute before normalizing time, so elapsed_time_s starts at 0 after trimming.

In [1]:
import plotly.io as pio
pio.renderers.default = "notebook_connected"
In [2]:
from pathlib import Path

import nymeria_gaze_tools as ngt
from nymeria_gaze_tools.preprocessing import (
    trim_recording,
    normalize_timestamps,
    convert_radians_to_degrees,
    remove_invalid_samples,
    compute_binocular_gaze,
    compute_confidence_widths,
    filter_low_confidence,
    compute_velocity,
)

DATA_ROOT = Path("../data/processed")

# Same example session used in individual_gaze_analysis.ipynb
SESSION = "20231128_s0_paul_nguyen_act0_3iqj71"

TRIM_START_MIN = 2.0
TRIM_END_MIN = 1.0

1. Load one raw session¶

Start from the raw eye gaze CSV. Preprocessing should always work on a copy, leaving raw untouched for comparison.

In [3]:
catalog = ngt.load_metadata(data_root=DATA_ROOT)
meta = catalog[catalog["sequence_uid"] == SESSION].iloc[0].to_dict()
raw = ngt.load_session(SESSION, data_root=DATA_ROOT)

duration_min = (raw["tracking_timestamp_us"].iloc[-1] - raw["tracking_timestamp_us"].iloc[0]) / 1e6 / 60
sampling_rate_hz = ngt.compute_sampling_rate(raw)

print(f"Session: {SESSION}")
print(f"Activity: {meta.get('script')} | Location: {meta.get('location')}")
print(f"Raw rows: {len(raw):,}")
print(f"Raw duration: {duration_min:.2f} min")
print(f"Sampling rate: {sampling_rate_hz:.2f} Hz")

Session: 20231128_s0_paul_nguyen_act0_3iqj71
Activity: S7-Cooking | Location: Loc_42
Raw rows: 11,842
Raw duration: 19.73 min
Sampling rate: 10.00 Hz

2. Trim the recording¶

Trim before timestamp normalization. That way, the first retained sample becomes time 0 in the later elapsed_time_s column.

In [4]:
trimmed = trim_recording(
    raw,
    trim_start_min=TRIM_START_MIN,
    trim_end_min=TRIM_END_MIN,
)

trimmed_duration_min = (trimmed["tracking_timestamp_us"].iloc[-1] - trimmed["tracking_timestamp_us"].iloc[0]) / 1e6 / 60

print(f"Rows after trim: {len(trimmed):,}")
print(f"Trimmed duration: {trimmed_duration_min:.2f} min")
print(f"Rows removed: {len(raw) - len(trimmed):,}")

Rows after trim: 10,040
Trimmed duration: 16.73 min
Rows removed: 1,802

3. Normalize timestamps¶

Add elapsed_time_s, measured in seconds from the first retained sample.

In [5]:
df_time = normalize_timestamps(trimmed)

df_time[["tracking_timestamp_us", "elapsed_time_s"]].head()
Out[5]:
tracking_timestamp_us elapsed_time_s
0 658610122 0.000000
1 658710106 0.099984
2 658810090 0.199968
3 658910074 0.299952
4 659010058 0.399936

4. Convert radians to degrees¶

The raw gaze angles and confidence bounds are stored in radians. Most downstream functions use degree columns.

In [6]:
df_deg = convert_radians_to_degrees(df_time)

degree_columns = [
    "left_yaw_deg",
    "right_yaw_deg",
    "pitch_deg",
    "left_yaw_low_deg",
    "left_yaw_high_deg",
    "right_yaw_low_deg",
    "right_yaw_high_deg",
    "pitch_low_deg",
    "pitch_high_deg",
]

df_deg[degree_columns].head()
Out[6]:
left_yaw_deg right_yaw_deg pitch_deg left_yaw_low_deg left_yaw_high_deg right_yaw_low_deg right_yaw_high_deg pitch_low_deg pitch_high_deg
0 0.748078 12.291298 -0.851917 -0.696588 2.029907 10.929425 13.692343 -2.589126 1.160292
1 3.549235 13.401519 2.048222 1.919070 4.966351 11.955279 14.867398 0.193080 4.310671
2 3.765394 13.786474 1.157722 2.287146 5.080221 12.343213 15.263578 -0.633979 3.277060
3 2.917514 12.204563 0.742118 1.518139 4.152245 10.822744 13.616386 -1.026510 2.875458
4 2.263542 9.270679 -2.046944 0.548419 3.844077 7.927475 10.671656 -3.949893 0.175188

5. Remove invalid samples¶

Drop rows with missing values before deriving binocular gaze and velocity.

In [7]:
df_valid = remove_invalid_samples(df_deg)

print(f"Rows before removing invalid samples: {len(df_deg):,}")
print(f"Rows after removing invalid samples: {len(df_valid):,}")
print(f"Rows removed: {len(df_deg) - len(df_valid):,}")

Rows before removing invalid samples: 10,040
Rows after removing invalid samples: 9,826
Rows removed: 214

6. Compute binocular gaze¶

Average left and right yaw into avg_yaw_deg. Pitch is already shared across eyes in this dataset.

In [8]:
df_gaze = compute_binocular_gaze(df_valid)

df_gaze[["left_yaw_deg", "right_yaw_deg", "avg_yaw_deg", "pitch_deg", "depth_m"]].head()
Out[8]:
left_yaw_deg right_yaw_deg avg_yaw_deg pitch_deg depth_m
0 0.748078 12.291298 6.519688 -0.851917 0.309666
1 3.549235 13.401519 8.475377 2.048222 0.361707
2 3.765394 13.786474 8.775934 1.157722 0.355150
3 2.917514 12.204563 7.561038 0.742118 0.384482
4 2.263542 9.270679 5.767111 -2.046944 0.512214

7. Compute confidence widths¶

Confidence widths summarize how wide the model confidence interval is for yaw and pitch.

In [9]:
df_conf = compute_confidence_widths(df_gaze)

df_conf[["yaw_confidence_width_deg", "pitch_confidence_width_deg"]].describe()
Out[9]:
yaw_confidence_width_deg pitch_confidence_width_deg
count 9826.000000 9826.000000
mean 8.151263 8.971467
std 8.266563 5.522517
min 2.079876 3.378698
25% 3.469491 5.547625
50% 4.929672 7.526441
75% 9.240109 10.390802
max 104.094820 58.953572

8. Optionally filter low-confidence samples¶

Leave the thresholds as None to match the default preprocess() behavior. Set one or both thresholds to drop samples with unusually wide confidence intervals.

In [10]:
MAX_YAW_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG = None
MAX_PITCH_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG = None

if MAX_YAW_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG is not None or MAX_PITCH_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG is not None:
    df_confident = filter_low_confidence(
        df_conf,
        max_yaw_width_deg=MAX_YAW_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG,
        max_pitch_width_deg=MAX_PITCH_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG,
    )
else:
    df_confident = df_conf.copy()

print(f"Rows before confidence filtering: {len(df_conf):,}")
print(f"Rows after confidence filtering: {len(df_confident):,}")
print(f"Rows removed: {len(df_conf) - len(df_confident):,}")

Rows before confidence filtering: 9,826
Rows after confidence filtering: 9,826
Rows removed: 0

9. Compute gaze velocity¶

Velocity is computed from the elapsed time axis and the preprocessed yaw/pitch signals.

In [11]:
df = compute_velocity(df_confident)

df[["elapsed_time_s", "avg_yaw_deg", "pitch_deg", "yaw_velocity_deg_s", "pitch_velocity_deg_s", "angular_velocity_deg_s"]].head()
Out[11]:
elapsed_time_s avg_yaw_deg pitch_deg yaw_velocity_deg_s pitch_velocity_deg_s angular_velocity_deg_s
0 0.000000 6.519688 -0.851917 19.560018 29.006031 34.984913
1 0.099984 8.475377 2.048222 11.283035 10.049804 15.109780
2 0.199968 8.775934 1.157722 -4.572424 -6.531563 7.972978
3 0.299952 7.561038 0.742118 -15.046524 -16.025893 21.982427
4 0.399936 5.767111 -2.046944 -32.317229 -14.783295 35.537995

10. Compare with the one-call helper preprocess() function¶

The step-by-step result we did can also be obtained quickly by invoking preprocess() when using the same trim and confidence settings.

In [12]:
df_one_call = ngt.preprocess(
    raw,
    trim_start_min=TRIM_START_MIN,
    trim_end_min=TRIM_END_MIN,
    max_yaw_confidence_width_deg=MAX_YAW_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG,
    max_pitch_confidence_width_deg=MAX_PITCH_CONFIDENCE_WIDTH_DEG,
)

print(f"Step-by-step rows: {len(df):,}")
print(f"One-call rows: {len(df_one_call):,}")

Step-by-step rows: 9,826
One-call rows: 9,826

11. Plot the preprocessed recording¶

Now the trimmed, preprocessed data is ready for visualization or event detection.

In [13]:
ngt.plot_gaze_timeseries(
    df,
    meta=meta,
    title=f"Preprocessed gaze after trimming {TRIM_START_MIN:g} min start / {TRIM_END_MIN:g} min end",
).show()
